topic: A person discovers their child has the same teacher they had
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp autumn air did little to cool the heat rising in my chest. I stared at the enrollment form, the ink blurring under my gaze. Sarah. The name swam before my eyes, a cruel, mocking echo of the woman who had shaped my son’s early years. I felt a sudden, sharp tightening in my jaw. How could this be? My daughter, little Maya, was about to enter the same classroom, under the same watchful eye, as my own child? I slammed the paper onto the desk, the sound echoing too loudly in the quiet living room. "This is absurd," I muttered, my voice tight. I walked to the window, gripping the sill until my knuckles were white, watching the familiar route of the school bus, a feeling of profound unfairness settling heavily over me.

[story 2]

I hadn't expected the discovery to hit me with such a physical force. It was a slow, creeping realization, starting with a misplaced name on a newsletter, then escalating into a full-blown internal tremor. When I finally saw the photograph—my child, a tiny version of myself, standing beside a woman whose face I knew intimately—a cold wave washed over me. I stood up so abruptly my chair scraped backward across the hardwood floor. "No," I breathed out, the word thin and brittle. I paced the length of the hallway, my movements jerky and agitated. Every glance toward the school building felt like a physical assault. I needed answers, needed to confront the reality of this overlap, but all I could manage was a furious, silent tightening of my fists at my sides.

[story 3]

The administrative office smelled faintly of old paper and stale coffee, a scent that suddenly seemed suffocating. When the secretary handed over the updated class roster, my breath hitched. There, listed under the new intake, was Ms. Evans. My heart began to hammer against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm. I took the sheet, my fingers trembling slightly, and didn't bother to thank her. I simply folded the paper with unnecessary force, the sound sharp and decisive. "This simply cannot be," I stated, my tone dangerously level, though the tension radiating off me was palpable. I turned on my heel before she could respond, the need to process this impossible coincidence overriding any politeness. I needed space to absorb the sheer ridiculousness of the situation.
